data.title: Cracker Barrel raises full-year adjusted EBITDA outlook to $215M–$225M as transformation plan advances
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cracker Barrel Old Country Store (CBRL) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Julie Felss Masino, President and CEO, stated the company was "pleased with our third quarter performance, which included positive comparable store restaurant sales for the fourth consecutive quarter and adjusted EBITDA that exceeded our expectations." Masino attributed these results to the effectiveness of the transformation plan and highlighted actions taken to support the top line and manage expenses in response to a soft start to the quarter.
  • Masino described culinary highlights, including spring menu promotions with two shrimp dishes and expanded pancake options, as well as the launch of Phase 1 of the back-of-house optimization initiative. She indicated a 14 percentage point improvement in hourly turnover and a 2.3% increase in internal net sentiment scores compared to the prior year quarter.
  • Masino announced the partnership with Speedway Motorsports and the Cracker Barrel 400 NASCAR race, describing it as a launchpad for the Campfire meals promotion, which returned for the first time since 2018. She said, "We've elevated the flavors, improved the quality and made them easier for the kitchen to execute."
  • Cracker Barrel Rewards program reached its fiscal '25 target of 8 million members, with over one-third of tracked sales now associated with loyalty members. Masino added, "We're focused on enhancing our personalization capabilities to further drive incrementality," mentioning AI-driven personalization tests that produced a mid-single-digit lift in average revenue per member.
  • On tariffs, Masino explained, "approximately 1/3 of our retail products are sourced directly from vendors in China," and outlined mitigation steps through vendor negotiations, alternate sourcing, and pricing, while accelerating SKU rationalization and adjusting seasonal promotions.
  • CFO Craig Pommells stated, "For Q3, we reported total revenue of $821.1 million, which was up 0.5% from the prior year quarter." He also noted, "Comparable store restaurant sales grew by 1%, while comparable store retail sales decreased by 3.8%."

Outlook

  • Pommells provided updated guidance for fiscal 2025: "We expect the following: Total revenue of $3.45 billion to $3.5 billion, pricing of approximately 5%, commodity inflation in the mid-2% range and hourly wage inflation in the mid-2% range."
  • The company raised its EBITDA outlook: "We increased our EBITDA outlook and now anticipate full year adjusted EBITDA of approximately $215 million to $225 million, which includes the previously mentioned $5 million tariff impact."
  • Pommells expects a "full year GAAP effective tax rate of negative 17% to negative 11% and an adjusted effective tax rate of negative 6% to 0%."
  • Capital expenditures are anticipated to be $160 million to $170 million.

Financial Results

  • Total revenue for Q3 was $821.1 million, with restaurant revenue at $679.3 million and retail revenue at $141.8 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $48.1 million or 5.9% of total revenue, compared to $47.9 million or 5.9% in the prior year.
  • GAAP earnings per diluted share were $0.56 and adjusted earnings per diluted share were $0.58.
  • Off-premise sales were 19.1% of restaurant sales.
  • Restaurant cost of goods sold was 26.2% of restaurant sales, and retail cost of goods sold was 48.9% of retail sales.
  • Labor and related expenses were 37.1% of revenue, down from 37.8% in the prior year.
  • The Board declared a quarterly dividend of $0.25 per share.

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Daniel Farmer, Gordon Haskett: Asked for clarification on Q4 trends versus Q3's 1% same-store restaurant sales. Pommells responded, "we're definitely seeing improving trends...we're particularly pleased that, that improvement continued further into Q4. So we're not giving an exact number other than to say that we're pleased with the Campfire promotion and it's resonating with guests."
  • Farmer inquired about expense management in Q3. Pommells explained, "we timed expenses in a number of areas, particularly around G&A...reduced our expense."
  • Todd Morrison Brooks, The Benchmark Company: Asked about G&A as a percent of sales for out-years. Pommells replied, "as we work through the transformation plan, that G&A will return -- as a percent of sales will return to closer to its historical levels."
  • Brooks questioned pricing and mix. Pommells stated, "The check was up 6.6% for quarter. That includes 4.9% of pricing, 1.7% of mix."
  • Jake Rowland Bartlett, Truist Securities: Asked about improved margin outlook amid sustained sales outlook. Pommells cited labor improvements and efficiencies from the back-of-house initiative.
  • Bartlett asked about tariff mitigation. Masino detailed SKU rationalization, vendor negotiations, alternate sourcing, and thoughtful pricing as key mitigation strategies.
  • Brian Hugh Mullan, Piper Sandler: Asked if the back-of-house optimization would permanently reduce labor hours. Masino and Pommells confirmed ongoing cost savings are expected as the initiative progresses.
  • Sara Harkavy Senatore, BofA Securities: Asked about traffic trends and demographics. Pommells said, "Our over 55 cohorts performed similarly to our under 55 cohorts, our over $60,000 income cohort performed similarly to our under $60,000."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on the sustainability of sales improvements, expense management, and the impact of tariffs, reflecting a neutral to slightly skeptical tone, especially regarding traffic trends and cost pressures.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, emphasizing progress on transformation, cost control, and strategic initiatives. Pommells and Masino frequently acknowledged challenges but reiterated confidence in ongoing momentum and strategy.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remains optimistic but more focused on cost containment and tariff mitigation, while analysts' tone has shifted to more probing on expense control and future margin resilience.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for total revenue and capital expenditures remained consistent, while the adjusted EBITDA outlook was raised from $210 million–$220 million to $215 million–$225 million, now including a $5 million tariff impact.
  • Management continues to highlight transformation initiatives such as back-of-house optimization and brand refinement, but there is an increased emphasis on mitigating external risks like tariffs.
  • Analysts shifted focus from holiday-driven sales and margin gains in Q2 to concerns about expense sustainability, tariff impacts, and labor efficiencies in Q3.
  • The tone from management remains constructive, with slightly more caution given external pressures, while analysts have become more cautious and inquisitive regarding the resilience of improvements.

Risks and Concerns

  • The tariff situation presents a risk, with approximately one-third of retail products sourced from China. Management outlined mitigation via vendor negotiations, alternate sourcing, SKU rationalization, and selective pricing.
  • Cost pressures from commodity inflation and wage increases are being managed through productivity initiatives.
  • The company faces ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty and potential consumer softness, with management noting the need for adaptation and continued focus on value.

Final Takeaway

Cracker Barrel’s management continues to emphasize momentum in its transformation plan, including enhanced product offerings, digital engagement, and operational efficiencies, while acknowledging and actively mitigating external risks such as tariffs and commodity inflation. The company has raised its full-year adjusted EBITDA outlook and remains confident in delivering on strategic priorities as it moves into the final quarter of fiscal 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Zimmer Biomet sued for violating Treace Medical patents related to bunion treatment
data.description:
• Treace Medical Concepts (NASDAQ:TMCI) on Thursday announced it sued Zimmer Biomet Holdings (NYSE:ZBH), alleging that the MedTech giant infringed four patents related to its Lapiplasty 3D bunion correction technology.
  • The lawsuit filed in a Delaware District Court seeks an injunction against Zimmer Biomet (ZNH) and potential damages.
  • The subject of the suit, Treace Medical’s Lapiplasty 3D Bunion Correction System, is made up of instruments, implants, and surgical methods designed to correct bunion deformity, a joint condition affecting nearly 67M Americans.
  • “We believe that this lawsuit is necessary to protect the significant investments we have made in our pioneering research and technology, which is in the best interest of our stockholders,” said John Treace, founder and CEO of the company.

data.title: Tesla sheds more than $100 billion in market cap after Elon Musk and Donald Trump go to war
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) fell sharply in Thursday trading after the relationship between President Trump and Elon Musk fractured in a public way.

Trump expressed “disappointment” over Musk’s opposition to the tax bill pending that is pending in the U.S. Senate and attributed it to the electric vehicle tax credit being pulled. That set off a social media rampage from Musk, criticizing the tax bill and its projected negative impact on the U.S. deficit.

Shares of Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) were down 8.5% at 1:10 p.m. after being down more than 9.4% earlier in the session. The selling pressure over the last two days has wiped out more than $100 billion from Tesla's (TSLA) market cap after taking into account yesterday's decline as well.

Elsewhere, Lucid Group (LCID) is down 4.0%, while Rivian Automotive (RIVN) is slightly positive. Both stocks already trade in the shadow of the EV tax credit development. Under current law, the EV tax credit, worth up to $7,500 for new EVs and $4,000 for used EVs, was set to expire at the end of 2032. The new bill accelerates the expiration to December 31, 2025, for most vehicles. This means that in 2025, the credit would only remain available for vehicles from manufacturers that have sold fewer than 200,000 qualifying EVs since 2010. Major brands, like Tesla (TSLA) and General Motors (GM), will see the credit would effectively disappear after 2025 if the bill is passed.

data.title: SA Asks: What are the best REIT stocks right now?
data.description:
What are the best REIT stocks right now for investors?

We asked Seeking Alpha analysts David Johnson and REITer's Digest for their picks.

David Johnson: The best REITs to invest in will have proven track records, executives that manage risk well, and are able to read the macroeconomic signals to their advantage.

For equity REITs, I recommend including STAG Industrial (NYSE:STAG), which has a high occupancy rate in existing warehouse space; CTO Realty Growth (NYSE:CTO), which has quality shopping centers with strong anchors such as Dick’s Sporting Goods or Whole Foods; Mid-America Apartment Communities (NYSE:MAA), an investment grade housing REIT; and Extra Space Storage (NYSE:EXR), the leader in self-storage, which benefits from house moves.

For mortgage REITs, I recommend investing in two hybrids, Starwood Properties (NYSE:STWD) and Ladder Corp. (NYSE:LADR). In both cases, management handles risk well, and the funds include buildings or properties that shelter some interest rate risk.

For investors that prefer a fund of REITs, I recommend Cohen & Steers Real Estate Opportunities and Income Fund (NYSE:RLTY).

REITer's Digest:  Current market conditions demand an emphasis on quality. REITs are facing headwinds including systemic issues like higher borrowing costs and pocketed challenges such as oversupply in key markets. As investors face a scenario where long-term interest rates remain higher for longer, certain REITs have suffered more than others. 

REITs with higher leverage, such as Global Net Lease (NYSE:GNL) or SL Green (NYSE:SLG) continue to face more pressure as their higher debt loads become more arduous with elevated capital costs. Similarly, REITs in oversupplied asset classes like Alexandria Real Estate Equities (NYSE:ARE) are treading water. 

In contrast, lower leverage REITs like Agree Realty (NYSE:ADC) and REITs in demand like data centers continue to outperform the market as their earnings growth charges ahead. Today, quality remains a top consideration in listed real estate.

  • Top Multi-Family Residential REITs Stocks
  • Top Industrial REITs Stocks
  • Top Healthcare REITs Stocks
  • Top Retail REITs Stocks
  • Top Diversified REITs Stocks
  • Top Office REITs Stocks
  • Top Self-Storage REITs Stocks

data.title: Circle Internet stock more than doubles in trading debut
data.description:
Circle Internet (NYSE:CRCL) stock opened at $69 in early afternoon trading on Thursday, more than double its initial public offering price of $31.

The volatility was so great that it was halted on volatility at 12:39 PM ET. Soon after trading started, Circle (NYSE:CRCL) stock jumped to $88.88.

The company behind stablecoin USCoin (USDC-USD) upsized its IPO to 34.0M of shares. The company offered 1.8M shares of class A common stock and selling stockholders offered 19.2M shares.

data.title: Spotify teams with United Airlines for in-flight streaming
data.description:
Passengers on United Airlines (NASDAQ:UAL) can now tune into video and audio podcasts, playlists, and audiobooks offered by Spotify (SPOT) for free onboard United’s (NASDAQ:UAL) Bluetooth-enabled aircraft.

Spotify (SPOT) content will be available on more than 680 United (NASDAQ:UAL) aircraft with the use of personal headphones and through passengers’ personal devices on Starlink-equipped aircraft.

"Spotify has a huge audience and people love their content. And now our customers can enjoy it all in a simple and easy way, at 35,000 feet," said Richard Nunn, CEO of United’s MileagePlus.

Although Spotify (SPOT) offers limited content on flights operated by Emirates and Delta Air Lines (DAL), the collaboration with United (UAL) is the first time the streaming service has offering in-flight audiobooks and video podcasts.

As part of the partnership between United (UAL) and Spotify (SPOT), the carrier is offering 1,000 bonus miles through its MileagePlus program to new subscribers to Spotify’s Premium Individual plan.

With a gain of more than 1%, Spotify (SPOT) shares have claimed a new record high Thursday.  

Source: Press Release

data.title: U.S. stocks rise after Trump and Xi Jinping's 'very good' phone call
data.description:
Wall Street on Thursday overcame a topsy-turvy open to push higher, after U.S. President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping held a "very good phone call" in which they agreed to visit each other. 

Market participants also digested back-and-forth between Trump and Tesla (TSLA) chief executive and former federal employee Elon Musk.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.3% in midday trade, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.4%, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.3%.

“I just concluded a very good phone call with President Xi, of China, discussing some of the intricacies of our recently made, and agreed to, Trade Deal. The call lasted approximately one and a half hours and resulted in a very positive conclusion for both Countries. There should no longer be any questions respecting the complexity of Rare Earth products. Our respective teams will be meeting shortly at a location to be determined,” said Trump on Truth Social.

“The conversation was focused almost entirely on TRADE. Nothing was discussed concerning Russia/Ukraine, or Iran. We will inform the Media as to scheduling and location of the soon to be meeting,” he added.

Over in the bond market, yields rose. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was up 3 basis points at 4.39%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) also advanced 3 basis point to 3.91%.

On the economic front, the U.S. trade deficit narrowed sharply in April as imports plunged.

"The temporary surge in imports as businesses pulled-forward demand to get ahead of tariffs has run its course. The U.S. international trade deficit narrowed sharply in April as a result and suggests a big boost to Q2 growth from net exports," Wells Fargo's Shannon Grein said. 

Also, initial jobless claims rose more than expected in the past week, while productivity growth was revised lower in the final Q1 estimates, and labor costs extended their climb.

The U.S. services sector leads in May job cuts, according to the Challenger Report.

Wall Street on Wednesday closed little changed, while bonds rallied after private hiring in May reached its lowest level in over two years.

The main event in terms of labor data—the monthly employment reading—will land on Friday.

data.title: Lands' End outlines $1.33B–$1.45B revenue target while expanding licensing and market reach
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lands' End (LE) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Andrew J. McLean highlighted the company's ongoing execution of a customer-centric strategy, with emphasis on "creative engagement, viral moments centered around the reimagining of our iconic totes, expansion of our brand through licensing and, of course, fresh solutions-based product." McLean reported improvements in supply chain resiliency and noted, "most pleasing was the continued performance at the top and bottom of our P&L with growth in GMV, which was low single-digit positive when adjusted for prior year inventory sell-off and a 12% improvement in our adjusted bottom line as we continue to flow through higher levels of incremental profitability, all accomplished on a faster inventory turn and with improved working capital." He attributed a record gross margin rate just shy of 51% to disciplined promotional activity and supply chain changes.
  • McLean described a strategic shift in sourcing, stating, "less than 8% of our purchase order dollars last fiscal year were utilized on buys out of China," and emphasized the company's agility in mitigating tariff impacts by accelerating production in the Western Hemisphere.
  • The CEO underscored the impact of digital and experiential marketing, referencing the "Tote Girl summer campaign" and the launch of a new AI-driven recommendation engine along with enhancements to SMS marketing, which generated nearly 400,000 new subscribers in Q1.
  • On the product front, McLean stated, "Wanderweight and Squall outerwear franchises were key winners for us in the quarter as were our Wear Now items like our Anyweather fleece and barn coats. Women's bottoms, knits, sweaters and dresses also performed successfully."
  • Licensing revenues increased by over 60% year-on-year, and new agreements were negotiated for travel accessories, men's underwear, base layers, and women's intimates. New licenses for hosiery and cold weather accessories were executed in Q2. McLean characterized licensing as "a significant vehicle for growth of the Lands' End brand."
  • The B2B Outfitters business met revenue and profit objectives, with a partnership launched with Delta Air Lines and $13 million in new school uniform business driven by competitor exits.
  • McLean reported "significant growth across our Amazon, Macy's and Nordstrom's marketplaces," crediting proprietary AI tools for marketplace optimization.
  • CFO Bernard Louis McCracken stated, "For the first quarter, total revenue performance came in at $261 million, a decrease of 9% compared to last year. When excluding the impact of transitioning the kids and footwear inventory to licensees in the first quarter 2024, total revenue decreased by 4% year-over-year."
  • McCracken noted a gross margin of 51%, an increase of approximately 210 basis points from the prior year. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $10 million, and the adjusted net loss was $5.4 million or $0.18 per share.

Outlook

  • Annual guidance remains unchanged, with net revenue expected to be between $1.33 billion to $1.45 billion, and GMV forecasted for mid- to high single-digit growth. Adjusted net income guidance is $15 million to $27 million and adjusted diluted earnings per share is projected at $0.48 to $0.86. Adjusted EBITDA guidance is $95 million to $107 million.
  • McCracken stated, "For the full year, our guidance includes the impact of tariffs at 30% for China and approximately 10% for the rest of the world. We are implementing mitigation measures to effectively manage the tariff headwinds at these levels."
  • No quarterly guidance was provided due to near-term tariff uncertainty.

Financial Results

  • Gross profit decreased by 5% compared to last year, while U.S. e-commerce sales and gross profit were flat year-over-year due to strong outerwear performance, offset by a delayed swim season.
  • Sales from Lands' End Outfitters increased 1% year-over-year, while the school uniform channel was slightly down, attributed to order timing.
  • Third-party marketplace gross profit dollars declined by 11%. European e-commerce sales decreased by 28% as the region underwent a strategic relaunch.
  • Licensing revenues increased over 60% year-over-year.
  • SG&A expenses decreased by $4 million year-over-year, though as a percentage of sales increased to 47% due to deleverage from lower revenues.
  • Inventory at quarter end was $262 million. Share repurchases totaled $3 million for the quarter.

Q&A

  • Dana Lauren Telsey, Telsey Advisory Group: Asked for quarterly guidance and color on tariff impacts. McCracken replied that annual guidance reflects current tariff rates and "we feel strongly that we have the right mitigations to get -- to offset those for the year." McLean added, "we rolled up our sleeves actually towards the end of Q4 and started shifting heavily into Western Hemisphere."
  • Telsey congratulated the company on the Delta agreement and inquired about the marketplace and European strategies. McLean said, "Nordstrom continues to be the fastest growth marketplace for us," and expressed bullishness on European expansion, particularly in France.
  • Marni Shapiro, The Retail Tracker: Asked about segment performance, site modernization, and customer transition from totes to other products. McLean described ongoing product innovation and noted, "the tote opens you up to sell the swim, to sell the tote, to sell the swim dress," and identified "swim dresses" as a key transition point for new customers.
  • Eric Martin Beder, SCC Research: Inquired about licensing strategy and performance of kids and footwear licenses. McCracken confirmed new licenses are "purely incremental." McLean noted strong results in kids and ongoing efforts to refresh the shoe assortment. Beder also asked about the Delta contract cadence and back-to-school impact. McCracken clarified the Delta contract is "just the normal run rate," and back-to-school effects will be seen in Q2.
  • Steven Silver, Argus Research: Asked about enterprise pipeline hesitancy and customer file expansion. McLean stated, "we saw very consistent business" in enterprise and detailed significant growth in 1x to 2x buyers, highlighting the success of new marketing strategies.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts asked mostly constructive, practical questions but pressed for details on near-term guidance and tariff impacts, reflecting a neutral to slightly cautious tone.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive tone throughout, with McLean emphasizing, "we rolled up our sleeves" and "we feel very positive" about marketplace and international opportunities. During Q&A, management continued to project assurance, especially around mitigation of tariffs and supply chain agility.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence remained steady, while analysts shifted focus from broad strategic execution to more tactical questions about mitigation and segment performance as macro uncertainty increased.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for annual revenue, GMV, adjusted net income, EPS, and EBITDA was reiterated from the previous quarter.
  • Strategic focus shifted to accelerated supply chain diversification away from China and further expansion of licensing into new product categories.
  • Analysts in the current quarter emphasized risk mitigation (tariffs, Europe relaunch), while prior quarter questions focused more on licensing growth and marketing innovation.
  • Gross margin improved to just shy of 51% from 46% in the most recent prior quarter, reflecting continued disciplined promotional activity.
  • Sentiment among management remained confident, but analysts in Q1 2025 showed increased concern about macro risks and tactical execution, compared to a more positive previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing tariff headwinds, with planned mitigation through accelerated Western Hemisphere sourcing and a diversified supplier base.
  • European e-commerce performance remains challenged, attributed to strategic repositioning, but is expected to benefit from recent relaunches and market expansion.
  • Analysts raised questions on the lack of quarterly guidance and the impact of tariffs on pricing and inventory, signaling near-term uncertainty.

Final Takeaway

Lands' End management emphasized continued progress on supply chain agility, margin expansion, and brand development through licensing and digital engagement. The company is maintaining its full-year guidance despite macro headwinds, citing proactive steps to mitigate tariffs and accelerate growth in both core and emerging channels. Strategic alternatives, including a sale or merger, remain under review, with management focused on building long-term shareholder value through innovation and operational discipline.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump tariffs estimated to reduce U.S. deficits by $2.8T - CBO
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs implemented between Jan. 6 and May 13 are estimated to reduce federal deficits by $2.8T over the next decade, the nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office said on Thursday, dismissing concerns that the trade war could lead to bigger deficits.

The retaliatory tariffs imposed by other countries in response to higher U.S. trade barriers are expected to weigh on economic output, which the CBO said will reduce federal revenues, "and higher nominal prices will increase federal revenues because taxes are levied on nominal income. The net result of those effects will be an increase in federal revenue."

Giving him extra firepower in justifying his impossible-to-predict trade policy, Trump reacted favorably to the report in a post on Truth Social, saying: "too bad this information couldn’t have been released earlier, it would have kept people from knowingly saying untruths."

Before factoring in how the tariffs would impact the economy's size, the CBO projected that higher levy revenues -- based on how trade flows would shift under the new tariff structure as of May 13 -- would cut primary deficits by $2.5T.

By reducing the federal government's need to borrow, the new tariff revenues would lower interest payments by another $0.5T, the document said. Taken together, and assuming no effect on the overall economy, the tariff changes would narrow total deficits by $3T.

On Wednesday, the CBO estimated that Trump's tax-and-spending package -- narrowly passed by House Republicans last month and now facing resistance in the Senate -- would add $2.42T to fiscal budget deficits over the next 10 years.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: De Beers draws interest from ex-CEOs as Anglo American starts sale - Bloomberg
data.description:
Anglo American (OTCQX:AAUKD) (OTCQX:NGLOD) is set to begin the formal sales process for its De Beers unit after it received interest from potential buyers including two former CEOs of the diamond company, Bloomberg reported Thursday.

Former De Beers CEOs Gareth Penny and Bruce Cleaver are both leading groups that are potential buyers, according to the report, which added that the company also has attracted attention from Australian mining veteran Michael O'Keeffe, according to the report.

The De Beers sale is the final step in Anglo's (OTCQX:AAUKD) (OTCQX:NGLOD) sweeping restructuring plan aimed at refocusing on core assets like copper and iron ore; the company is also weighing options for an IPO or demerger of De Beers, but a sale is the preferred and most likely option, the report said.

Anglo (OTCQX:AAUKD) (OTCQX:NGLOD) has waited to sell De Beers as the diamond industry faced a crisis after a collapse in Chinese demand and fierce competition from synthetic diamonds; Anglo wrote down the value of the unit for a second time in February, to ~$4.9B.
